nativeRange.habitat: Coral reefs and adjacent rubble/sand zones at depths of roughly 1–25 m; adults often seen in pairs over open substrate near reef edges.

decorPreferences: live rock with caves and crevices

diet.notes: Has been documented feeding on small fishes and benthic invertebrates in the wild. In captivity it tends to accept mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, chopped seafood, and other meaty frozen preparations; generally advised to offer meaty foods 2–3 times daily, especially while juveniles are settling in.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 3460x2595. Tack-sharp, vibrant subadult on a clean sandy background showing the bright yellow dorsal fin, dark facial stripe through the eye and white saddle patch; whole fish framed.
Diego Delso; 3795x2530. Sharp adult on plain substrate clearly showing the two dark facial lines, yellow dorsal edge and silver-white body; clean diagnostic side profile.
Jens Petersen; 3264x2448. Sharp adult in a reef setting showing the yellow-edged upper facial line, dark bridle and white belly; different individual and angle for variety.

summary: An Indo-Pacific spinecheek bream with a bold white-and-yellow body and two dark facial stripes; generally considered hardy and an active sand-sifting predator best suited to large fish-only or FOWLR aquariums.
careNotes: The Two-line Monocle Bream is an active, semi-aggressive predator that spends much of its time near the substrate, sifting sand in search of small crustaceans and worms, and will opportunistically take small fish and motile invertebrates that fit in its mouth — for that reason it is generally considered not reef-safe with delicate invertebrates. A deep sand bed and open swimming areas are typically recommended, alongside rockwork with caves for retreat. Juveniles display a strikingly different color pattern of bold yellow-and-black stripes, believed to act as a Batesian mimic (a harmless species that mimics the appearance of a dangerous one) of the venomous fang blenny Meiacanthus grammistes; this juvenile coloration fades as the fish matures. The species has been reported to jump, so a tight-fitting lid is generally advised. Best kept singly, as conspecifics are typically intolerant of each other in home-aquarium volumes.
breedingNotes: Reported as a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite that spawns pelagically in the wild during warmer months. Captive breeding is generally considered impractical in home aquariums.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic broadcast spawning in the wild during warmer months; not known to be bred in home aquariums.
